## I Asked DeepSeek to Clean Up Our Cloud - It Flagged 3,000 Resources for Deletion

**Pillar:** AI Automation Scripts
**URL:** https://www.praveentechworld.com/blog/deepseek-cloud-cleanup-flagged-3000-resources
**Published:** 2026-06-29

> A DeepSeek-generated AWS cleanup script identified 3,000 resources as deletable. The AI hallucinated tag matching logic. Safety checks prevented a disaster.

I recently tasked DeepSeek with crafting a Python script to help me find orphaned cloud resources in an effort to trim our AWS bill. You would think this would be a straightforward task, right? Well, the first version of the script flagged an astonishing 3,000 resources for deletion   way off from the estimated 20 true orphans. Let's dive into what happened, and how I managed to fix it.

What Problem Made Me Build This?
You know that creeping feeling when your AWS bill keeps climbing each month? That's exactly what I was dealing with. I had a hunch that orphaned resources, like forgotten EC2 instances, detached EBS volumes, and outdated snapshots, were hiding out there, accumulating costs like dust bunnies under a couch.

While exploring AWS Config and Trusted Advisor, I found both tools could identify unused resources. However, they often return a ton of false positives. In my case, I needed something that would truly understand our specific tagging conventions and how our teams operate.

Honestly, manually sifting through 11 AWS accounts across two regions would have taken ages. I was looking for a neat little script that could:

1. Scan all 11 accounts and both regions.
2. Identify EC2 instances, EBS volumes, load balancers, and snapshots with no recent activity.
3. Cross-reference against our tagging system to pinpoint actual owners.
4. Generate a clear report of what could be safely deleted.

So, I thought, why not let DeepSeek handle the heavy lifting? Sure, I can whip up basic boto3 calls, but navigating complex multi-account auth and API calls was a bit much for me.

How I Prompted It

I crafted a detailed prompt to help DeepSeek understand what I was aiming for. Here's what I said:

After running the script with , I was met with a staggering 900 lines of code. But that's where things started to get a bit dicey.

Where Did the AI Output Break?
The script had some major hiccups that could've led to serious issues. Let's break it down:

Hallucinated Tag Matching Logic

The AI assumed that any untagged resources would show up as , which is a classic misunderstanding of the AWS API. In reality, if a resource has no tags, the  field just shows up as an empty list. The script reported zero untagged resources, which in hindsight should have raised a red flag. I was too focused on the output numbers to notice the inconsistency.

To make matters worse, the AI added a filter that checked for  and , flagging anything that was missing either one. However, our organization has a different tagging structure. Some teams use  instead of , while others prefer  over . So, while the AI's logic was functioning, it simply didn't align with our environment.

False Positive Cascade

Since the tag matching didn't work, the script couldn't properly determine the ownership of numerous resources, defaulting to "deletable" for anything it couldn't classify. This resulted in 3,012 flagged resources   a number that left me reeling. 

Upon inspecting the CSV, I found that it had flagged:

12 running EC2 instances actively serving traffic but missing the 'Project' tag.
4 production load balancers that seemed idle during maintenance windows.
2,800 EBS snapshots that were over 90 days old   many from decommissioned accounts, but around 30 were still in active use.

Among the flagged resources, only about 200 were actual orphans. Finding them amongst the sea of false positives? Nearly impossible.

No Pagination (Again)

The script only scanned the first page of results for each API call. AWS APIs paginate results, and for accounts with hundreds of resources, the script was only considering about 20% of the real inventory.

Rate Limiting from Cross-Account Assumption

The script sequentially assumed IAM roles for each account without any backoff. When you switch roles too quickly, the AWS API throttles you, and boom   the script crashed on the fourth account with a . No partial results, no retry.

What I Had to Fix

I spent several evenings redesigning the script to tackle these issues.

Tag matching (2 hours): I replaced the AI's binary check with a configurable tag mapping. Now, the script reads a  that defines which tags correspond to ownership for each account. 

Idle detection (1 hour): I improved the logic to check CloudWatch metrics for actual network traffic instead of just the instance state. A stopped instance behaves differently from a running-but-idle instance, and I introduced a configurable idle threshold.

Snapshot filtering (1.5 hours): I added a check against AWS Backup to see if each snapshot is part of a backup plan, which cut false positives from 2,800 down to just 40.

Pagination (1 hour): I implemented pagination loops using AWS's built-in paginators, ensuring that each resource type was properly handled.

Cross-account rate limiting (30 minutes): I added a random delay between 2-5 seconds between account switches to avoid throttling.

Output (30 minutes): The CSV output was enhanced to group resources by account

---

## I Built a DeepSeek API Cost Tracker and Saved $2K a Month

**Pillar:** AI Automation Scripts
**URL:** https://www.praveentechworld.com/blog/deepseek-api-cost-tracker-scripts
**Published:** 2026-06-28

> An IT Ops Lead used DeepSeek to build a Python script tracking AI API costs across providers. The AI hallucinated endpoints. Manual fixes saved $2K/month.

I recently set out on a journey to build a Python script that tracks our AI API costs across four different providers using DeepSeek. After a week of late-night debugging (thanks to some interesting AI 'hallucinations'), I managed to save my team about $2,000 per month in unnecessary spending. Curious about how it all unfolded? Let me share my experience!

The Problem That Started It All

So, here's the deal: our team was burning through AI API credits faster than I could keep track. We had engineers using OpenAI for various tasks, a research project leveraging Anthropic Claude, my own experiments with DeepSeek, and the data team diving into Google's Gemini. The end result? Financial chaos!

Invoices were arriving in different formats at totally unpredictable times, and when I finally added everything up, I was shocked $4,700 per month! Almost half of that went to premium models for tasks that really didn't need them.

What I needed was a command-line tool that could:
1. Pull usage data from all four providers
2. Normalize costs into a single view
3. Identify our most expensive models
4. Generate a simple HTML dashboard for the team

This felt like a job for DeepSeek! While I only have basic Python skills, I thought having AI handle the tedious work of wiring together four different API SDKs with authentication and cost normalization was a smart move.

Building the Tracker with DeepSeek

I decided to craft a detailed prompt for DeepSeek, hoping to generate a solid foundation to build upon:

To my surprise, DeepSeek generated about 800 lines of code using standard libraries like  and . It even included a cute animated spinner for data fetching. I thought, "This is too good to be true!" 

Where the AI Code Fell Apart

But when I ran  with my actual API keys, the issues began to surface.

Imaginary API Endpoints

First, I discovered that DeepSeek had completely fabricated OpenAI's usage API endpoint:

This URL doesn't exist! OpenAI's usage data actually comes from their billing dashboard API or CSV exports. The AI generated something that looked plausible but was purely fictional.

Cost Calculation Errors

Then, I noticed that the script assumed all providers used the same pricing formulas. In reality, it was way more complicated:
Anthropic rounds token counts per request
Google returns costs in micros (millionths of a cent)
DeepSeek provides token counts but no cost data

Pagination Problems

When I tested it with real production data, it only captured the most recent 100 records from each provider. Each API handles pagination differently, which the AI code overlooked completely:
OpenAI uses cursor-based pagination
Anthropic uses offsets
Google uses page tokens
DeepSeek uses limit/offset

Rate Limit Chaos

With 12 API keys across four providers, the script's basic  approach didn't cut it. We quickly hit:

The single retry attempt after 5 seconds was insufficient for our actual usage patterns.

The Fixes That Made It Work

After six evenings of debugging, here's what I changed:

API Endpoints (2 hours): I replaced all fictional endpoints with the real ones from each provider's documentation.

Cost Calculations (1 hour): I built provider-specific cost calculators with hardcoded pricing tables (as of June 2026) and added a  flag to keep them updated.

Pagination (2 hours): I implemented proper pagination for each provider using generator patterns to handle their different approaches.

Rate Limiting (1 hour): I swapped the basic sleep for a proper token-bucket system that respects each provider's limits and rotates through available keys.

Dashboard (30 minutes): I rewired the AI-generated HTML template to use our actual normalized cost data instead of the hallucinated numbers.

The final version consisted of three files:
 (600 lines) Main CLI
 (500 lines) Provider-specific logic
 (300 lines) HTML generation

Testing against 90 days of real usage showed costs within 3-5% of provider dashboards.

The Real Savings

With visibility into our costs, the waste became painfully clear:
OpenAI GPT-5: A forgotten background job using GPT-5 instead of GPT-4-mini $800/month
Anthropic Claude Opus: Research team using Opus when Sonnet would suffice $600/month
DeepSeek: Double-paying through a 30% markup aggregator $400/month
Google Gemini: Unlimited test accounts running expensive experiments $200/month

Total savings: around $2,000/month!

Lessons Learned

This project taught me some valuable lessons about working with AI-generated code:

1. AI can invent plausible-looking APIs That two-hour wild goose chase for a non-existent endpoint was a real eye-opener. The AI can be confidently wrong.

2. Pricing changes frequently Hardcoded cost tables can go stale fast. The  flag became essential.

3. Rate limiting can get complex quickly What works for one key may fail spectacularly with multiple. A proper token bucket system solved the issue.

4. Pagination is a silent killer Without proper handling, you'll miss much of your data an

---

## I Automated Server Health Checks with DeepSeek

**Pillar:** AI Automation Scripts
**URL:** https://www.praveentechworld.com/blog/automated-server-health-checks-with-deepseek
**Published:** 2026-06-26

> I used DeepSeek to generate a Python health check script. The first version had critical bugs. Here is the exact prompt and the fixes.

I recently decided to automate my server health checks using DeepSeek, and let me tell you, it was quite the journey! The first version of the script had some major hiccups that would have caused serious problems under real server loads. After some debugging and adjustments, I now have a lightweight health checker that monitors four servers and only alerts me when there's an actual issue. 

What Made Me Want to Do This?
I manage a handful of Ubuntu servers for various projects. One of them is dedicated to monitoring, another houses a small e-commerce site, and the other two are for internal tools for clients. Every Saturday morning, I found myself SSHing into each server and running the same commands:

This manual process was a bit tedious and took about 15 minutes. I kept telling myself that I should set up Nagios or Zabbix, but honestly, that felt like overkill for just four servers. I wanted something simpler a single Python script that I could run from my laptop to check all servers, gather the metrics, and alert me if anything was off.

The Prompt I Gave to DeepSeek

I opened up DeepSeek and crafted a single prompt that captured everything I wanted:

DeepSeek generated this impressive 450-line script with all the features I asked for, including async SSH connections and threshold checks. It even came with a colorful summary table! The output cost me around $0.002 on DeepSeek's API, which isn't too shabby.

What Went Wrong Initially

Unfortunately, the first version had a few critical issues:

1. SSH Connection Pooling: While the AI used asyncssh, it created a fresh connection for every single metric check. On the first run, my laptop opened 12 simultaneous SSH connections to each server, which caused my client's server to flag it as a brute force attempt and block my IP. I received an error message that read:
   

2. Threshold Logic Was Inverted: The script used OR logic for triggering alerts, which meant if one metric was high (like CPU), it would alert even if all the others were fine. This led to me getting bombarded with 47 identical Slack messages within 10 minutes!

3. No Error Handling on SSH Failures: When one server was down for maintenance, the script crashed entirely instead of skipping it and checking the others. The unhandled exception caused the whole loop to stop.

   

My Fixes

After realizing all these issues, I made four key improvements:

1. Connection Reuse: I rewrote the SSH logic to open one connection per server, run all the checks through that single connection, and then close it. This reduced the number of connections from 12 to just 3 per cycle.

2. Alert Deduplication: I added a mechanism to track sent alerts by server and metric. If an alert had already been sent within the last hour, it would skip it. This change reduced my Slack notifications from 47 to just 1.

3. Graceful Failure Handling: I wrapped each server check in a try/except block to log any failures and continue with the others, so one down server wouldn't derail the entire process.

4. Parallel Execution: I retained DeepSeek's async structure but switched from asyncio.gather() to asyncio.wait() with return_when=FIRST_EXCEPTION, allowing me to collect results even when some servers failed.

The final script is now 4.1KB and checks four servers in about 6 seconds (a significant improvement from the original 45 seconds). Plus, memory usage remains under 40MB!

The Working Result

Here's a sneak peek of what the output looks like when I run the script:

The configuration file is simple and looks like this:

What I Learned About Crafting Prompts for Health Check Scripts

1. Be Clear About Connection Management: I learned that DeepSeek assumed every metric needed its own SSH connection, so grouping them together in a single statement helped a lot.

2. Deduplication Needs to Be Explicit: I had to clarify in the prompt that I wanted to track which alerts had been sent to avoid duplicates.

3. Consider Unreachable Servers: The AI didn't initially account for what happens when a server is down, so adding that to the prompt was crucial.

4. Cost vs. Time: While DeepSeek's output cost me $0.002, it took me 90 minutes to debug and fix the issues. Writing everything from scratch would have taken about 3 hours, so I saved some time, but there were trade-offs.

The Exact Prompt

In case you want to experiment with DeepSeek yourself, here's the unedited prompt I used:

FAQ

Q: Does this script work on Windows servers'  
A: Not directly. The script uses /proc and subprocess calls that assume a Linux environment. For Windows, you would need to swap the disk check to wmic or PowerShell Get-CimInstance and change the service check to Get-Service.

Q: Can I run this without email or Slack configured'  
A: Yes. Omit the  webhook flag and the script logs everything to health.log and stdout. You can pipe the output to any other tool.

Q: How much CPU does this use'  
A: On a 2-core VPS, the script uses about 0.8% CPU during each check

---

## I Built a Log Monitor with DeepSeek. Here Is Where It Broke.

**Pillar:** AI Automation Scripts
**URL:** https://www.praveentechworld.com/blog/i-built-a-log-monitoring-script-with-deepseek-here-is-what-went-wrong
**Published:** 2026-06-25

> The short answer is: I built a log monitoring Python script using DeepSeek, but the generated code hallucinated and needed a lot of manual fixing.

I built a Python log monitoring script using DeepSeek, but boy, did the generated code need some serious fixing. Let me walk you through my journey from messy log files to a working solution complete with the exact prompt you can use for your own experiments!

The Problem That Kept Me Up at Night

Every morning, I found myself scrolling through , trying to spot new issues. My servers were generating hundreds of errors overnight, and I needed a way to know when things went south quickly. The pattern was clear: if I saw more than three errors in a 5-minute window, I wanted an alert. 

What I needed was a lightweight Python script that could:
Tail the log file in real-time
Count errors in rolling windows
Send a ping to my Slack whenever the thresholds were crossed
Run smoothly on any Ubuntu box without heavy dependencies

After a week of coding manually, I thought, "Why not ask AI for help  So, I turned to DeepSeek to generate the complete workflow, hoping to learn how to prompt AI for real automation tasks.

My DeepSeek Prompt (and What Came Back)

I crafted a detailed prompt that covered everything from log parsing to sending Slack notifications. Here's exactly what I provided to DeepSeek:



Surprisingly, DeepSeek returned 560 tokens (about 3.8KB) of polished code. It had colored output, used  properly, and even included daemon functionality. For comparison, OpenCode's output was more compact but completely messed up the sliding-window logic.

Where the AI Code Failed

The first red flag popped up when I ran the script on a clean VM:



Here were some other issues I stumbled upon:
1. The sliding-window logic reset after each check rather than maintaining state.
2. The webhook payload had non-serializable datetime objects.
3. OpenCode's version entirely omitted the webhook functionality.
4. Neither version properly handled missing dependencies.

The cost for using DeepSeek was minimal (around $0.0018 for the API call), but the debugging time? That really added up.

How I Fixed the AI's Code

I took DeepSeek's output as a starting point and made these key improvements:

1. Dependency checks I added clear error messages when  or  were missing.
2. Proper windowing I fixed the deque logic to maintain error counts across checks.
3. JSON fixes I converted datetimes to ISO strings before the webhook calls.
4. Graceful degradation Made colored output optional if  isn't available.
5. Better daemonizing Switched to  for proper PID file handling.
6. Robust logging Added log rotation to avoid disk bloat.

The final script runs in 0.32 seconds for initial load and uses minimal CPU (under 2% on a 2-core server).

Key Lessons Learned

1. AI is like a junior dev Great for first drafts but really needs careful review.
2. Test in clean environments What works on your dev machine might fail elsewhere.
3. Prompt specifically My detailed prompt helped, but I still had to add "check for missing packages."
4. Log everything Proper logging saved me hours of debugging.
5. Cost vs. quality DeepSeek's output was more useful despite the small cost.

The fixed script has been running smoothly on my production servers for a month now. It's not enterprise-grade (you'd likely want to add proper rate limiting and TLS verification for that), but it does solve my immediate problem.

Try It Yourself

Here's my exact DeepSeek prompt if you want to give it a shot:



FAQ

Q: What packages do I need'  
Just  and  (plus  if you're using the daemon flag). The script will let you know if anything's missing.

Q: No Slack webhook'  
No worries  the script will still monitor and count errors without it.

Q: How's the performance'  
It's lightweight, taking about 0.015 seconds per check cycle on my servers.

Q: Is it production-ready'  
It works for my needs, but for critical systems, you might want to enhance the error handling.

What do you think? What would you automate with this approach'

References & Further Reading

Wikipedia: Log file
DeepSeek

## I Automated TLS Renewal with DeepSeek

**Pillar:** IT Operations & Infrastructure
**URL:** https://www.praveentechworld.com/blog/automated-tls-certificate-renewal-with-deepseek
**Published:** 2026-06-23

> The short answer is I automated TLS certificate renewal using a DeepSeek‑generated Python script, but it almost broke production due to missing.

I want to share a little adventure I had automating TLS certificate renewal with a Python script generated by DeepSeek. It was a wild ride, and I'll admit it almost caused a production outage. Let me tell you what happened!

Why I needed automation

Every 90 days, I would spend my Sunday afternoons manually renewing TLS certificates across three Nginx servers. You can probably guess how that went:
1. SSH into each server
2. Backup the current configs
3. Run 
4. Restart services
5. Repeat for each domain

It was such a tedious process, and I was always worried I might miss an expiration date. My monitoring system would alert me if any certs expired, but I wanted something that would just handle it all automatically at 2 AM, so I wouldn't be jolted awake at 3 AM.

Getting DeepSeek to write the first draft

So, I decided to give DeepSeek a shot. I fed it a detailed prompt because, you know, the more context you give, the better the output:

The initial response was about 284 tokens long, which seemed promising. It even had a proper argparse setup and logging. I saved the script as  and tested it locally. It worked fine in about 12 seconds, and the dry-run mode produced sensible output. Everything seemed ready for production!

When the script failed spectacularly

I set up a cron job to run weekly at 2 AM:

But then, at 3 AM, I received a panicked call. The script failed immediately with an . Digging deeper, I found a few critical issues:

1. The  function was completely fake it always returned "45 days from now," so renewals never triggered.
2. Missing Python dependencies () on production servers.
3. Inconsistent Nginx service names across servers ( vs ).
4. Permission issues with log files.

It was clear that the AI had provided me with a convincing prototype that crumbled under real-world conditions.

How I fixed it

After realizing what had gone wrong, I spent about four hours transforming the prototype into something production-ready with three key improvements:

1. Real dependency management
I added a  and automatic dependency checking:

2. Actual certificate checking
I replaced the placeholder logic with real certbot/openssl calls:

3. Robust safety features
I added:
Proper error handling
Configurable Nginx service names
JSON output for monitoring
Better logging
Dry-run improvements

The final product

My production-ready script now:
1. Checks for required dependencies upfront
2. Uses real certificate expiration checks
3. Handles different Nginx service names
4. Provides detailed logging
5. Outputs machine-readable summaries
6. Has a comprehensive dry-run mode

To run it, you can do:

Key lessons learned

1. AI needs very specific instructions My initial prompt didn't clearly specify real certificate checking, so the AI just provided a placeholder.
2. Production is different from development Missing dependencies and environment differences can really break things.
3. Safety features are mandatory The dry-run mode and good logging saved me hours of debugging.
4. Monitoring is still needed I set up alerts to catch any failures in the automated process.
5. Cost-effective The AI prompt cost me $0.04 and dependencies $0.02 much cheaper than my time spent on manual renewals!

The AI gave me about 80% of what I needed, but that last 20% required my judgment and real-world experience. I'd love to hear your thoughts! Have you tried something like this? For another example of this approach, check out how I automated server health checks.

Full prompt for reproduction

FAQ

Why did the first version fail'
Placeholder logic for certificate checks
Missing Python libraries in production
Environment differences between dev and prod

How to add more domains'
You can use either:
 CLI argument
JSON config file with a domain list

Need root access'
Yes, for reading certs and reloading Nginx.

How to monitor'
The script outputs JSON summaries that can be fed into your monitoring system.

Safe testing'
Use the  flag to simulate without making changes.

What would you automate with this approach?

## I Asked DeepSeek to Build My Sysadmin Toolkit - It Failed 3 Times

**Pillar:** AI Automation Scripts
**URL:** https://www.praveentechworld.com/blog/i-asked-deepseek-to-build-my-sysadmin-toolkit-here-is-what-it-made-and-broke
**Published:** 2026-06-23

> The short answer is that I used DeepSeek to build a suite of Python scripts for log parsing, disk monitoring, and user management by providing.

I Asked DeepSeek to Build My Sysadmin Toolkit

So, I decided to give DeepSeek a shot at creating some Python scripts for tasks like log parsing, disk monitoring, and user management. I thought I could outline the logic and let the AI tackle the syntax. Initially, I was pretty excited it churned out about 80% of functional code. But, there were a couple of hiccups along the way, including a few imaginary methods and some head-scratching moments with Linux permissions that needed a bit of manual fixing. If you're curious about my other adventures with DeepSeek, check out my TLS certificate renewal script and log monitoring tool.

Why was I spending 5 hours a week on manual log audits?
As an IT Operations Lead, I find myself balancing high-level strategy with the nitty-gritty of system maintenance. For the past six months, I've been stuck manually parsing  and  to track down failed SSH attempts and monitor disk space. Honestly, it's tedious, and I felt like I was wasting my time.

I'm not a hardcore developer sure, I can read Python and run scripts, but crafting a robust CLI tool from scratch? That's my kryptonite. I've dabbled with other language models before, but I was curious if DeepSeek could manage the "messiness" of system-level automation without me having to micromanage every line of code.

My goal was pretty straightforward: build a "Sysadmin Toolkit" with three tools:
1. A Log Parser that flags security anomalies.
2. A Disk Monitor that alerts me when a partition hits 90%.
3. A User Management script to audit stale accounts.

I didn't want a tutorial I wanted a practical tool. So, I thought of DeepSeek as my junior developer. I would provide the architecture and business logic, and the AI would handle the syntax.

How did I prompt DeepSeek to build the toolkit?
I didn't kick things off with a vague "write me a script" prompt. I've learned from past experiments like when I tried to automate my server health checks that being specific is crucial. I gave the AI a persona and a detailed set of requirements.

Using the DeepSeek-Coder model, I crafted a comprehensive prompt that outlined the environment (Ubuntu 22.04), the required libraries (sticking to standard ones where I could), and the exact output format I needed (JSON for logs, plain text for alerts).

Here's the gist of my prompt:

Where did the AI hallucinate and break?
DeepSeek generated the code in a flash just 12 seconds. The indentation was spot-on, variable names were clear, and the  wrapper had this nice switch-case logic. I felt like a genius for about five minutes. Then, I actually ran the code.

The first hiccup happened with . The AI used a method called . When I ran the script, I was met with this error:
.

It turned out DeepSeek had invented a method. It treated the list of partitions as one object that could be queried for usage. In reality, I needed to iterate through the list and call  on each individual partition object. It looked good on paper, but it relied on a library method that simply didn't exist.

The second issue came from the  script. It tried reading  to check for the last login date. While that made sense logically, the script crashed with a . The AI didn't account for the fact that I needed to run the script with  privileges it just assumed it had root access.

The third problem was with the log parser. It worked, but man, was it slow! On a 400MB log file, it took 42 seconds to execute and gobbled up 1.2GB of RAM by reading the entire file into memory with . For a sysadmin tool, that's a disaster waiting to happen. If I were to run this on a production server with a 2GB log file, I'd likely trigger an OOM (Out of Memory) killer and crash the server.

What did I have to fix to make it production-ready?
Here's where my "IT Ops Lead" brain kicked in. I might not be the best at writing optimized Python, but I know how the system should behave. I went back to the AI, but instead of just asking it to "fix the code," I provided specific error messages and performance data.

For the , I shared the exact traceback.
For the , I explained: "The script crashes because it lacks root access. Please add a check at the start of the script to verify if the user is root using , and if not, exit with a helpful error message."

For the memory issue, I mentioned: "The current log parser is reading the entire file into memory, which isn't efficient for large files. Please rewrite the log parsing logic to use a generator or iterate through the file line-by-line to keep memory usage under 50MB."

Here's how the log parser logic looked before and after:

Before (AI's first attempt Memory Hog):

After (My corrected prompt Memory Efficient):

The execution time didn't change much, but the memory footprint dropped from 1.2GB to about 14MB. That's a huge difference between a script that works on my laptop and one that's ready for a production server.

What is the working result?
After three rounds of iterative prompting and one manual fix to t

## I Built a Database Audit Script with DeepSeek

**Pillar:** Build in Public
**URL:** https://www.praveentechworld.com/blog/non-developer-built-database-audit-script-with-deepseek
**Published:** 2026-06-23

> A non-developer IT Ops Lead used DeepSeek to build a database audit Python script. The exact prompt, three AI failures, and the working 20-line script.

What Problem Drove Me to Build a Database Audit Script?
So, here's the thing: I really needed a database audit Python script, and after three attempts with AI, I finally got it right. Every week, I find myself checking my Oracle and MySQL instances to spot oversized tables, unused schemas, and those annoying deprecated  columns. While I could run manual queries, it usually took me about 30 to 45 minutes, plus I had to copy and paste results into spreadsheets for my weekly compliance report. My team kept asking for an automated "audit-once-and-forget" CLI tool that could scan a list of databases, flag anomalies, and even email a PDF summary. The catch? I'm not a developer I'm just an IT Operations Lead who spends my days managing runbooks and monitoring dashboards. So, I thought, why not ask AI to help me out? I decided to give DeepSeek (and OpenCode) a shot, hoping for a simple script with just a few tweaks. What I got instead was a saga of coding chaos three failed attempts, a lot of frustrations, and finally, a working script after spending about 4 hours fixing it.

How I Asked DeepSeek and OpenCode to Write the Script (The Prompt)

For my first try, I crafted a straightforward prompt that outlined the business logic and requested a CLI tool. I saved this prompt in a markdown file, threw it into DeepSeek's web interface, and received a 30-line Python script. It looked beautiful, but it just wouldn't run. Here's the exact prompt I used (I kept it to 312 tokens, which DeepSeek said would cost me 0.004 USD):



The response came back in about 12.3 seconds, and the file size was 1.8 KB. I saved it as  and ran . The help text displayed nicely, but when I tried to run it against a dummy config, the script crashed with . The prompt had said to "assume the required DB drivers are installed," but clearly, that assumption didn't hold up. That was my first failure.

Where the AI Attempts Broke: Three Failures I Faced

Failure #1 Missing Drivers  
DeepSeek's script assumed a perfect environment. Unfortunately, my test VM only had  and  installed. The  import blew up. I tried to install it via , but it turns out that this package requires Oracle Instant Client libraries, which I didn't have set up on this Linux container. Here's what the error log looked like:



Failure #2 Hallucinated Schema Queries  
After removing the Oracle check to make the script runnable, I asked DeepSeek for a "quick fix" to substitute the Oracle block with a placeholder. The new script came back with queries like . The issue? That table doesn't exist in MySQL! The AI mistakenly dropped Oracle-specific queries into the MySQL script. When I ran it, I got this delightful error:



Failure #3 Incorrect JSON Structure  
On my third attempt, I asked DeepSeek to "just give me a working script that outputs the findings as a CSV instead of JSON." The AI obliged, but the 28-line script it generated used  to write CSV without importing it first. So, of course, it crashed with . Plus, the CSV header I received was , which didn't match my team's requirements for a nested JSON array. They flat-out rejected the output because they needed the exact JSON format for their downstream reporting pipeline.

Through these failures, I learned a valuable lesson: the AI can produce visually appealing code, but it often assumes an environment that may not exist, and it can mix vendor-specific syntax without any warning. My next approach was to design the prompt around constraints and validate the output before fully trusting it.

What I Had to Fix: Human Intervention and Debugging

For my fourth attempt, I switched up my strategy. I asked DeepSeek to create smaller functions that I could test individually. I also added a "validation step" in the prompt: "Please include a simple  block that prints a sample output so I can verify the JSON shape." I also requested that the script detect missing drivers and skip the DB with a clear warning.

Here's the revised prompt I used (token count 378, cost $0.0051):



DeepSeek returned a 22-line script (file size 1.2 KB). When I first ran it, it printed a warning for Oracle and proceeded with MySQL and PostgreSQL. The JSON output looked correct, but then I noticed the timestamp was a naive string instead of ISO‑8601. My team's downstream parser expected . I edited the script to use . I also added a  flag to suppress warnings if users wanted cleaner output. The final script ran in 2.3 seconds on my test VM, processing three databases, and produced a clean JSON file of 3.4 KB.

Here's a before-and-after comparison:

Before (AI‑only) broken snippet:


After (my fix) working snippet:


The script now runs as needed, handling missing drivers and producing the correct JSON output.

How the Final Script Works: Result and Output

When I ran  in my lab environment (Ubuntu 22.04, Python 3.10), here's what popped up in 2.3 seconds:



The  file (size 3.4 KB) contained:



Oh, and there

---

## Core Web Vitals Failing' 7 Fixes to Save Your Rankings

**Pillar:** Website Setup & SEO
**URL:** https://www.praveentechworld.com/blog/core-web-vitals-fail-new-website-improve-lcp-inp-cls
**Published:** 2026-06-18

> If your new website fails Core Web Vitals before publishing, fix LCP, INP, and CLS now. Here are 7 urgent fixes to protect your rankings.

A Common Dilemma

So, you've just built your shiny new website, but it's failing Core Web Vitals? That can be pretty frustrating, right? If you're scratching your head wondering how to tackle this, you're not alone. The good news is that there are some actionable steps you can take to improve your site's performance before it goes live.

Here's What I Found

Core Web Vitals measure how users experience your website. They assess how quickly the main content loads, how responsive the page is to user interactions, and how stable the layout is while loading. Specifically, we're looking at three key metrics: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP), Interaction to Next Paint (INP), and Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS).

If your site is struggling, it's often due to large images, slow server responses, or layout shifts. Testing tools like PageSpeed Insights can help you pinpoint these issues. 

Step-by-Step Breakdown

Here's a friendly guide to help you tackle these Core Web Vital issues. Let's dive in!

1. Test Your URL: Start by testing the exact URL you plan to publish on PageSpeed Insights. It'll give you a clear picture of LCP, INP, and CLS scores, along with other important metrics. I often jot these down to track my progress.

2. Make the Staging Site Accessible: If your staging site is password-protected, consider temporarily lifting the restrictions so testing tools can access it. This can often be adjusted in your site's settings.

3. Optimize Your Hero Image: Your hero image can significantly impact LCP. Check the image size and format. Aim for a smaller size (around 120-220 KB for desktop and 60-140 KB for mobile) and consider using formats like WebP or AVIF for better compression.

4. Avoid Lazy Loading for Key Images: Make sure your main hero image isn't set to lazy load. This can delay its appearance and negatively affect LCP. Set the width and height attributes for the image to avoid layout shifts.

5. Streamline CSS and JavaScript: Heavy scripts can block rendering. Use tools like Chrome's Lighthouse to identify which scripts can be deferred or removed. In WordPress, plugins can help manage this.

6. Eliminate Unnecessary Scripts: Review your plugins and third-party tools. Sometimes chat widgets or sliders can add significant loading time. Consider loading them after user interaction instead of on the initial page load.

7. Stabilize Your Layout: To reduce CLS, set width and height attributes for all images and reserve space for ads and embeds. This helps avoid content jumping around while the page loads.

8. Check Font Loading: If you're using custom fonts, ensure you're not loading too many font weights. Two should suffice for most sites. Use  in your CSS to ensure text appears promptly.

9. Simulate Real User Experience: Use Chrome DevTools to mimic how your site looks on mobile devices. Interact with the site to see if you notice any layout shifts or delays.

10. Retest After Changes: After making adjustments, go back to PageSpeed Insights to see how your changes have impacted your scores. Tackle one major issue at a time to track improvements accurately.

Limitations: This Won't Work If...

These tips might not solve your issues if your hosting server is slow, or if there's something blocking the test tools from accessing your site. If your Time to First Byte is over 800 milliseconds, you might need to look into better hosting or a closer CDN.

What's Your Take?
I'm always curious to hear how others tackle these issues. Have you faced challenges with Core Web Vitals? What strategies have worked for you? Let's chat in the comments!

References & Further Reading

Google Web Vitals
Wikipedia: Core Web Vitals

---

## Sitemap Submitted but Pages Stay Undiscovered?

**Pillar:** Website Setup & SEO
**URL:** https://www.praveentechworld.com/blog/sitemap-submitted-but-pages-undiscovered-robotstxt-check
**Published:** 2026-06-18

> Your sitemap says submitted successfully but pages stay undiscovered? Check robots.txt, internal links, and canonicals to fix it.

Direct Answer

Have you ever submitted a sitemap only to find that some pages remain undiscovered? You're not alone! Just because your sitemap shows as 'submitted successfully' doesn't guarantee that Google will index every page. Think of it more like a suggestion rather than a command. In my experience, the first things to look at are robots.txt, weak internal links, and canonical tags that might be misdirecting Google. If those look good, then it's worth checking page quality, redirects, noindex tags, and any server response issues.

Explanation

This can be a bit confusing when you're navigating Google Search Console. You submit your XML sitemap, Google accepts it, and everything seems okay. But then you check the Pages report and find URLs marked as Crawled currently not indexed, Discovered currently not indexed, or Excluded by 'noindex' tag.

The main purpose of a sitemap is to inform Google about the existence of your URLs and how often they're updated. It doesn't force Google to crawl, index, or rank them. Google's own documentation explains this well: Sitemaps overview.

When a page stays undiscovered, it could be for a few common reasons. Google may find the URL in the sitemap but then hit a wall with your robots.txt file. If a page lacks internal links, Google might see it as isolated. There could also be a canonical tag pointing to another URL, which tells Google to ignore the page you want indexed.

This issue often pops up with newly published blog posts, product listings, category pages, and promotional landing pages. The sitemap may look fine, but if the page isn't well-linked from your homepage or related posts, or if it has a canonical tag that's just wrong, it can be problematic. So, I like to think of sitemap status as a starting point in troubleshooting, not the final answer.

When This Fix Works

This fix is effective when Google can technically access the page but isn't crawling it due to mixed messages from your site. For instance, if your sitemap includes , but your robots.txt file blocks , Google sees the URL, checks the rules, and then decides to exclude it from crawling.

It can also help when pages lack internal links. If you've got a new page in your sitemap that isn't linked from key pages like your homepage or a related post, it may be sitting in a weak discovery state. From what I've seen, adding just a couple of contextual links from relevant pages can really help Google understand that this page is part of your site.

It's also relevant when canonical tags are incorrect. A common issue, especially with WordPress, is when a canonical tag points to the homepage or some unrelated page. If that's the case, Google might just follow that signal and ignore your desired page.

When This Does NOT Work

However, this fix won't work if the page is considered thin, duplicated, or not valuable enough for Google to bother indexing. Simply having a sitemap won't make a low-value page worth crawling. Even if everything looks technically correct, Google might pass over a page with only a few words of duplicated content.

Also, if the URL returns an error (like 404, 410, 500, or even a soft 404), then your sitemap is pointing Google to a page that doesn't exist properly. You'll want to resolve the page's status first.

This approach won't fix ranking issues either. A page can be indexed but still rank poorly due to weak content or competition from stronger pages. For that, you'll need to focus on improving content and SEO, not just fixing the sitemap.

And if your website is brand new, it might just need some time. New sites often experience slower initial crawl rates from Google. I typically wait 2 to 6 weeks before making any judgments about crawl behavior, assuming the technical setup is correct.

Step-by-Step Instructions

1. Open Google Search Console and select your website property from the menu on the left.

2. Click on Sitemaps under the Indexing section. Check the submitted sitemap URL, like . If it's incorrect, remove it and submit the right one.

3. Open the Pages report under Indexing. Look for the affected URL in Discovered currently not indexed, Crawled currently not indexed, or Not found (404). If the page isn't listed, paste the exact URL in the inspection field and hit Enter.

4. Run the URL Inspection test for the affected page. Check if Google says the URL is available. If it says it's blocked by robots.txt, you'll want to check that next.

5. Test robots.txt using Google's official tool. Head to the robots.txt testing tool and enter your URL. If it's blocked, check your robots.txt file at .

6. Remove any rules that block the page or section you want indexed. For example, change  to a more specific rule, or remove it altogether if you want all blog posts crawled.

7. Check the live page for a noindex tag. In your browser, right-click on the page and select View Page Source. Search for . If you find , you'll need to remove that from your page settings or SEO plugin.

---

## Sitemap URLs Blocked by Robots.txt? Clean and Resubmit

**Pillar:** Website Setup & SEO
**URL:** https://www.praveentechworld.com/blog/sitemap-urls-blocked-by-robotstxt-clean-and-resubmit
**Published:** 2026-06-18

> If Google Search Console says your sitemap URLs are blocked by robots.txt, clean up your sitemap and resubmit it in GSC with this guide.

Direct Answer

Have you ever found yourself staring at Google Search Console, only to see that your sitemap URLs are blocked by robots.txt? That can be a real head-scratcher! But don't worry I've got some tips to help you clean up your sitemap and resubmit it. The main idea is to remove any URLs from your sitemap that aren't meant to be there, especially those that are blocked, duplicate, private, or non-canonical. After that, you might need to tweak your robots.txt file, but only if it's blocking a useful page by mistake. Then, you can test the affected URLs in Google Search Console and resubmit your sitemap. Simple enough, right?
Explanation

So, what's going on here? An XML sitemap is essentially a wish list for Googlebot it's a list of URLs you'd like Google to find. On the flip side, robots.txt is a set of rules that tells Googlebot what it can and cannot crawl on your site. If there's a match between a Disallow rule in robots.txt and a URL in your sitemap, Googlebot can't crawl that URL, even though it's listed in the sitemap. 

According to Google Search Central, these robots.txt rules dictate what Googlebot can crawl. 

This kind of issue can pop up after you've made some changes to your CMS, migrated your site, or updated an SEO plugin. Suddenly, your sitemap might start including unwanted pages like tag pages, author pages, or even old URLs that should be long gone. 

For instance, if your sitemap includes , but your robots.txt has this rule:

Googlebot will be unable to crawl that URL. The fix? Just remove  from your sitemap. 

Another example is if you have a live page under , but robots.txt has:

In this case, your sitemap might be fine, but robots.txt is too broad. You would want to edit robots.txt to allow Google to crawl your actual blog pages. 

If you're still setting things up, it's a good idea to start with the basics.

When This Fix Works

This approach works well when your sitemap includes pages you genuinely want Google to crawl, but robots.txt is mistakenly blocking them. For example, if you have a real service page at , but robots.txt says , that's a problem you can fix! 

It's also helpful if your sitemap has junk URLs things that should never be there, like tag pages or staging URLs. After a site migration, if your old sitemap still lists  while robots.txt blocks , cleaning up the sitemap can resolve that conflict. 

The best outcome occurs when you address both sides: removing unwanted sitemap URLs and correcting overly broad robots.txt rules.

When This Does NOT Work

Now, there are some situations where this fix won't be applicable. If a page is private and should remain that way like an admin page or internal file you'll want to keep the robots.txt rule and just remove it from the sitemap. 

Similarly, if a page has a noindex tag and you want it indexed, you'll need to remove that noindex tag first. If the server returns errors like 403, 401, or 500, that's a different issue altogether it's a server or permissions problem, not a sitemap conflict. 

And if the canonical URL points elsewhere, Google might ignore your submitted URL in favor of the canonical version. For issues like that, you might want to explore further.

Lastly, if your sitemap file itself is broken returning 404, 500, or invalid XML Google Search Console won't be able to process it correctly.

Step-by-Step Instructions

Alright, let's break this down into some easy-to-follow steps:

1. Open Google Search Console and select your property.  
   Head over to Google Search Console. Make sure you pick the exact property for your site. If you have both  and , choose the one matching your live site.

2. Go to the Sitemaps report.  
   In the left sidebar, click on Sitemaps under Indexing. Check your submitted sitemap status. If it says "Success" but still shows blocked URLs, head over to the Pages report next.

3. Export the blocked URLs from GSC.  
   Click on Pages in the left sidebar. Look for the section called Why pages aren't indexed. You might see entries like "Blocked by robots.txt." Click the row, and use the export button in the top right corner of the table. Starting with the first 50 URLs is usually enough to spot patterns without overwhelming you.

4. Inspect a few blocked URLs.  
   Go back to the left sidebar and click URL Inspection. Paste one of the blocked URLs into the search box at the top. Hit TEST LIVE URL. If it says the page is blocked by robots.txt, you've got a crawl rule conflict.

5. Open your robots.txt file.  
   Open a new tab and go to , replacing  with your actual domain. If this file returns 404, Google treats it as if there are no rules, so the issue might be with your sitemap.

6. Match the blocked URL against the Disallow rules.  
   Use  on Windows or  on Mac to search within robots.txt. If the blocked URL is , look for rules like , , or any wildcard rules such as . 

7. Decide which side is wrong.  
   Ask yourself: should Google crawl this URL or not? If yes, you'll n

---

## GA4 Data Delayed or Missing' 10 Troubleshooting Steps

**Pillar:** Website Setup & SEO
**URL:** https://www.praveentechworld.com/blog/ga4-data-delayed-or-missing-check-measurement-id-consent
**Published:** 2026-06-17

> GA4 data delayed or missing after installation? Check Measurement ID, consent mode, filters, and Realtime before reinstalling the tag.

Direct Answer

Hey there! Are you struggling to find your GA4 data after installation? You're not alone! It can be frustrating when standard reports are empty. Before you go down the rabbit hole of reinstalling, let's take a moment to check a few things: the Measurement ID, consent settings, filters, and the Realtime report. If everything's set up correctly, you should see a test visit in Realtime.

Explanation

So, how does GA4 data actually flow? It's pretty simple: your site loads the Google tag, the browser sends events to Google, and then Google processes those events into standard reports.

But if something goes wrong along the way, those reports can come up empty. Common culprits include an incorrect Measurement ID, a tag that only loads on the wrong URL, cookie banners blocking analytics, browser privacy tools, or GA4 filters that might be excluding your traffic.

According to Google Support, while GA4 standard reports can take between 24-48 hours to process, the Realtime report should show recent activity pretty quickly: About data collection and processing in Google Analytics. That's why I always check Realtime first before jumping to conclusions about a failed install.

In my experience, the wrong Measurement ID is often the main issue. Sometimes, a site might still have an old ID like , while the current GA4 data stream uses something new, like . In such cases, the tag may load, but the hits end up going to the wrong property.

Consent issues are another sticky point. If your cookie banner prevents analytics from running until the visitor accepts cookies, GA4 won't record that visit unless you have the consent update configured correctly. This is especially common on platforms like WordPress, Shopify, and Webflow.

And let's not forget about filters, which can also hide your data. GA4 can exclude internal, test, and developer traffic from reports. If you've marked your home network as internal traffic and are testing from there, your visits might not show up.

When This Data Fix Works

This approach works when GA4 is installed correctly, but your reports are empty due to a setup issue. It's also effective when the tag is firing but your test traffic is being filtered out.

I've followed these steps on WordPress, Shopify, and static websites, and it tends to work well because it checks the data path from the page to GA4 rather than making guesses based solely on the reports.

When This Does NOT Work

However, this won't help if your website has no visitors or if the GA4 property is linked to the wrong domain. It also won't fix data that wasn't collected before the tag was installed.

If the tag is just missing from the live page or your site blocks  and , you'll need to address the page code or server rules first. And if your traffic is really low, standard reports might still appear empty, even when the tag is working.

Step-by-Step Instructions

Alright, let's dive into the troubleshooting steps!

1. Find the correct GA4 Measurement ID

   Open GA4 and click on Admin at the bottom left. Under Property, click on Data streams. 

   Select your website stream and copy the Measurement ID from the top-right. It should start with , like . Keep this page open for comparison with your website's code.

2. Check the ID on your live website

   Open your website in a new tab. Right-click on the page and select View page source.

   Press  (Windows) or  (Mac) and search for your GA4 Measurement ID, like .

   If you find the correct ID, great! If you only see an old ID, the site is sending data to the wrong GA4 property. 

   Sometimes, outdated SEO or cache plugins on WordPress might retain old code even after adding the new GA4 tag.

   If you're using GTM, check out How to Use Google Analytics 4 to Improve Your Content Strategy to make sure tracking is set up right before adding more tags.

3. Check the tag with Google Tag Assistant

   Go to tagassistant.google.com in Chrome and click Preview.

   Enter your website URL in the box on the left and hit the blue Connect button.

   Your site will open in a new tab. Return to the Tag Assistant tab and look for the green Tags Fired box.

   If you're using GTM, your Google tag should show up under Tags Fired. If it appears under Tags Not Fired, click it and check the trigger.

   One common issue is having a page view trigger set to  but being blocked by a consent rule. Another possibility is that the GTM container might be set on the wrong domain, like , while your live site is .

4. Check the GA4 Realtime report

   Head back to GA4. Click on Reports in the left menu, then Realtime.

   Open your website in a new tab and refresh the page once.

   In the Realtime report, look for Users per user property on the right. You should see at least one active user.

   If you see your visit in Realtime, that's a good sign! The tag is working, but standard reports might need a little more time to catch up.

   If Realtime is empty, that suggests the issue is still

---

## GA4 Shows Realtime Users but Reports Are Blank

**Pillar:** Website Setup & SEO
**URL:** https://www.praveentechworld.com/blog/ga4-shows-realtime-users-but-reports-blank-fix-processing
**Published:** 2026-06-17

> GA4 Realtime shows users but standard reports are blank? The tag is firing but data has not processed yet - here is how to fix event processing.

Direct Answer

If you're seeing users in GA4's Realtime but your standard reports are coming up blank, it might be due to unprocessed data, active filters, or some settings that just need tweaking. First, hang tight for about 24-48 hours to see if data gets processed. After that, check the date range, time zone, Data filters, and make sure  is showing in Events. Trust me, don't rush to reinstall the tag right away. Let's sort out the event processing and report settings before making any big changes.

Explanation

Here's the thing: Realtime reports give you live activity almost instantly, while standard reports rely on processed data that goes through various checks and rules. That's why you might see a couple of active users in Realtime, but your Acquisition, Engagement, and Pages reports say "No data available." The hit reached GA4, but it hasn't been processed or it's being hidden from view.

According to Google Support, GA4 report data usually gets processed within 24 hours, with acquisition source data possibly taking up to 48 hours (Google Support). I like to think of this timeframe as a first checkpoint, not a reason to panic.

I've encountered this issue on fresh WordPress sites where, after testing from an office IP address, the GA4 internal traffic rule was set to Active too soon. Realtime showed the test visit, but the standard reports stayed empty because that processed event was marked as internal traffic.

Another thing to watch out for is your report configuration. If you change the date to yesterday or add a comparison, the data might be there, but the report just isn't displaying it correctly.

So, let's take a methodical approach. Start by checking the stream, then event processing, followed by filters, and finally, report settings. If you need a hand with setup, my GA4 page tracking guide covers how page views work, and my GA4 events guide shows how I check events before making changes to reports.

When This Fix Works

This approach works when GA4 Realtime is showing visitors, page views, clicks, or other events, but standard reports are looking empty or incomplete. It's also helpful when Engagement has partial data, but Acquisition is blank because the traffic source attribution hasn't finished processing.

This is particularly effective for new sites, new GA4 properties, and those transitioning from Universal Analytics to GA4. Often, the tagging is fine; it just takes time and a clean configuration for the reports to catch up.

Additionally, if the problem stems from a GA4 filter like if your internal traffic rule blocks your office IP your test traffic might show up in Realtime but disappear from standard reports after processing.

When This Does NOT Work

However, this won't help if the tag is broken. If Realtime isn't showing any activity at all, the issue is likely with the GA4 Measurement ID, Google tag, Google Tag Manager container, cookie consent, ad blockers, or something in your site code.

It also won't address cases where you're looking at the wrong property. If you're checking a GA4 property for an old domain or a staging site, your fresh site data won't appear there.

And remember, if you're looking at a report with no eligible users like if your site had 8 visitors today but you check a report for last week GA4 isn't broken; it's just that the date range is off.

If the tag isn't firing at all, you might want to start with the GA4 troubleshooting steps before diving into changing GA4 report settings.

Step-by-Step Instructions

1. Open GA4 and confirm the property name.

   In the bottom-left corner of GA4, click Admin. In the Property column, click Property details. Make sure you're looking at the right property name, ID, and web data stream.

   If it says something like "Demo Account" or "Old Site," you might be in the wrong place. Go back to the property list and select the correct GA4 property.

2. Check the web data stream and Measurement ID.

   Still in Admin, head to the Property column and click Data streams. Click on the web stream for your site and look at the Measurement ID. It should look like .

   Compare that ID with the one on your website or in Google Tag Manager. If your site uses  but GA4 Realtime shows data under , you could be checking different properties.

3. Set the report date to Today.

   Open Reports on the left side of GA4. Click Engagement, then Pages and screens. In the top-right corner, click the date picker and set it to Today.

   I can't tell you how many times I've fixed blank GA4 reports just by ensuring the date range is set correctly. A fresh site can look empty if the date is set to yesterday or earlier than when GA4 was installed.

4. Check your GA4 time zone.

   Head to Admin. In the Property column, click Property settings. Look for the Time zone setting.

   If your GA4 property is set to Pacific Time but your server and visitors are in India, your "Today" report might not match your local day. 

---

## GA4 Traffic Numbers Look Wrong? Check 5 Settings

**Pillar:** Website Setup & SEO
**URL:** https://www.praveentechworld.com/blog/ga4-traffic-looks-wrong-check-consent-mode-and-filters
**Published:** 2026-06-17

> If GA4 traffic looks wrong after website setup, check three things first: Consent Mode, GA4 data filters, and cross-domain tracking.

A Common Dilemma

Have you ever looked at your GA4 traffic numbers and thought, "This can't be right  You're not alone! Many folks face this issue after setting up their website. But don't worry, I'm here to help you figure it out. Let's explore some easy settings to check first: Consent Mode, GA4 data filters, and cross-domain tracking.

Here's What I Discovered

From my experience, most of the time when people see "bad GA4 data," it's not just random. It usually comes down to a few key things, like a cookie banner blocking analytics, filters that might be excluding real users, or issues with tracking across different domains. Before you go reinstalling your analytics plugin or your Tag Manager, let's troubleshoot these settings!

GA4 is quite different from basic page load counters. It tracks users, sessions, events, consent states, and more. So, any changes made after you set everything up can cause your reports to show unexpected drops or splits in data.

Start with Consent Mode

Consent Mode is usually the first place I look. If your cookie banner sets  to denied before the GA4 tag has a chance to load, it could lead to incomplete data. I've seen this happen firsthand; one client saw a 36% drop in traffic right after a new cookie banner went live. The reason? Many visitors simply didn't click Accept for analytics cookies.

Check Your Data Filters

Misconfigured filters can also cause trouble. If your filters are too broad, they might accidentally exclude legitimate traffic like visitors from your internal team or developers. I recommend diving into Google's GA4 data filters documentation for more insights. I've encountered cases where an office IP was added correctly, but the filter ended up being too broad, removing visitors from the same network.

Cross-Domain Tracking Challenges

Cross-domain tracking can be tricky too. If it's not set up right, GA4 might mistake one visitor as two separate users one on your main site and another when they reach your checkout or payment pages. If the GA4 linker doesn't capture the client ID, it thinks it's a new session. I've seen websites lose significant conversions because of this.

Also, keep in mind that GA4 may take a day or two to settle down after you make changes. If you adjust your Consent Mode at 2 p.m. and check reports an hour later, you might still see a mix of old and new data.

It's worth noting that GA4 users and Google Search Console clicks are different metrics. They provide valuable insights but capture different aspects of user behavior. If you want to dive deeper into this, check out my post on Google Search Console Not Showing Data.

When This Fix Works

You'll find this fix helpful if your GA4 traffic changed right after setting up your website, launching a cookie banner, updating Tag Manager, or adding a new domain. It's also useful when traffic looks fine in Realtime but seems low in standard reports.

If you can replicate the problem, that's great! For example, try opening your site in a private window: decline cookies, then accept them, and check what GA4 records when moving to the checkout domain.

When This Doesn't Work

Just a heads-up: these solutions won't change historical GA4 data. Filters only impact reports after they're implemented, and changes to Consent Mode won't alter past data either.

Also, GA4 won't match other analytics tools exactly. Different tools like GA4 and server logs have their own rules, so I see server logs as a useful check, not an exact match.

And remember, this doesn't solve every privacy issue. Some visitors block scripts or use private browsing, which means you can't force every browser to send the same data.

Lastly, if you're facing attribution problems, GA4 may categorize traffic differently. In that case, you'll need to check your campaign tags and referrer settings too.

Step-by-Step Breakdown

1. Check Your GA4 Data Stream ID

   Open GA4 and click Admin in the lower left. Under Data collection and modification, select Data streams. Open your web stream and copy the Measurement ID (it looks like ).

   Next, check your website source, Tag Manager container, or analytics plugin to ensure the same  ID is used everywhere. If you find discrepancies, choose one as the main property and remove the duplicate.

2. Verify GA4 Tag Firing in Google Tag Manager

   Open Google Tag Manager and click Preview in the upper right. Enter your website URL and click Connect. 

   Navigate through your home page, a service page, and the main conversion page. On the left, check Tags Fired. Your GA4 Configuration tag should fire on All Pages. If it doesn't, fix that before diving into filters or cross-domain settings.

3. Check Consent Mode Before the GA4 Tag Loads

   Look at your cookie banner settings. Ensure it controls . If you're running ads in the EEA, also check , , and .

   In GTM, check Consent Overview. You want to see a default consent state before the page loads. If the GA4 tag fires before the cookie consen

---

## Understanding Sitemap and Indexing: Fix Crawling Issues in 2026

**Pillar:** Website Setup & SEO
**URL:** https://www.praveentechworld.com/blog/understanding-sitemap-indexing-fix-crawling-issues
**Published:** 2026-06-17

> Fix crawling issues by understanding how sitemaps and indexing work together. Step-by-step guide to get every page discovered and indexed.

Have you ever submitted your sitemap to Google Search Console, only to find that everything looks good, yet your pages are still MIA in search results? You're definitely not alone! This is one of the most common indexing headaches that many new site owners face.

So, what's the deal? A sitemap is like a friendly nudge to Google, letting it know which pages exist on your site. But just because Google sees your sitemap doesn't mean it will automatically index your pages. Indexing depends on various factors, like the quality of your content, how authoritative your site is, and some behind-the-scenes technical stuff. Let's dive in and explore how sitemaps and indexing work together, and how you can troubleshoot when things go awry.

How Sitemaps Work

Think of an XML sitemap as a roadmap for search engines. It lists all the URLs on your site that you want them to know about. You can usually find it at , and it looks a little something like this:

When Googlebot reads this file, it adds the URLs to its crawl queue, but don't expect them to be indexed right away. Instead, these URLs are scheduled for a visit later on. 

The Indexing Pipeline

The journey from submitting your sitemap to having your pages show up in search results involves four main stages:

1. Discovery: Google finds your URL (via sitemap, backlinks, or manual submission).
2. Crawl: Googlebot visits your URL and grabs the page content.
3. Processing: Google renders the page, extracts content, and assesses its quality.
4. Indexing: Google decides whether to add the page to its search index or not.

It's a common misconception that stages 2-4 happen automatically after submission. Each step has its own set of challenges.

Stage 1: Discovery

Your sitemap helps get your URLs into Google's system, but there are a few things that can trip you up:

Blocked by robots.txt. If your sitemap or its pages are blocked in , Google may discover those URLs but won't crawl them. I once accidentally blocked half my site by adding a  rule, so double-check yours!
  
Wrong sitemap URL. If you submitted  but your actual file is at , Google finds nothing. It's always good to double-check the filename and path in the Sitemaps report in Search Console.

Broken URLs in sitemap. If your sitemap lists URLs that lead to 404 errors, that's a waste of Google's crawl budget. You might want to use a sitemap validator tool to check for errors.

For a more detailed guide on submitting your sitemap correctly, check out the sitemap errors guide. And if your site is new, the add your site to Google Search guide covers everything you need to set up.

Stage 2: Crawling

Once Google has discovered your URL, it needs to crawl it. This means sending Googlebot over to your server, downloading the HTML, and processing it. Some common issues here include:

Server timeout. If your server takes too long to respond (more than a few seconds), Googlebot might give up and try again later. If this happens often, it can cause Google to deprioritize your site altogether.

5xx errors. Temporary server errors are okay, but if you keep getting persistent 500 or 503 errors, that signals to Google that your site isn't reliable. Fix those first!

Redirect chains. If URL A redirects to B, which then redirects to C, that's a lot of hops for Googlebot to follow. Try to keep redirects to a single hop to avoid wasting crawl budget.

You can check your server logs for Googlebot IPs and see what status codes they are receiving. If you spot any errors, tackle those server issues before diving into sitemaps.

Stage 3: Processing

After Googlebot has downloaded your page, it's time for processing. This is where many indexing issues can arise, and Google evaluates:

Content quality. Pages with less than 300 words of meaningful content often get skipped. Thin affiliate pages, auto-generated content, and pages heavy on images with minimal text can fail this check.

Uniqueness. If your content is similar to what's already out there, Google might choose to index just one version. Original research, unique insights, and personal experiences can help your page stand out.

Rendering. If your site relies heavily on JavaScript, Googlebot might not see the same content a user sees. You might want to test your pages with the URL Inspection tool to make sure Googlebot can access all the content.

Also, keep in mind that Google primarily uses the mobile version of your page for indexing. If your mobile site is broken or missing content, it won't help to have a perfectly optimized desktop version.

Stage 4: Indexing

Finally, we reach the indexing stage where Google decides whether to include your page in search results. If your page passes the first three stages but still doesn't get indexed, it might be due to:

Quality flags. Google's automated systems might flag your page as low quality based on thin content, too many ads, or content that resembles spam patterns.

Manual actions. In rare cases, a human reviewer might have flagg

---

## Website Speed Upgrades: Fix Crawling Delays and Core Web Vitals

**Pillar:** Website Setup & SEO
**URL:** https://www.praveentechworld.com/blog/website-speed-upgrades-fix-crawling-delays-and-core-web-vitals
**Published:** 2026-06-17

> Fix crawling delays by addressing the real causes of slow Core Web Vitals. Server config, image optimization, and caching strategies that actually work.

Have you ever noticed that Google seems to be crawling your site slower than you'd like? It's a frustrating issue, but it often boils down to your server response time. When your server lags, it can signal to Google that your site might not be a priority. Let me share some insights from my own experiences and how I tackled this challenge.

How Crawl Budget Depends on Speed

So, here's the deal: Google has limited resources for crawling websites. If your site is fast and reliable, it gets a larger slice of the crawl budget. On the flip side, if it's slow, it gets less love from Google. Even a tiny delay in how quickly your server responds can really affect how many pages Google decides to crawl.

For instance, if your Time to First Byte (TTFB) is over 800ms, Googlebot might slow down the crawl rate. But if you get it under 200ms, Google thinks, "Hey, this site can handle more traffic!" and increases the crawl frequency.

I saw this play out on my own site. Before I optimized my performance, Google crawled around 15-20 pages a day. After cutting my TTFB to under 300ms, that number jumped to 40-60 pages daily. It's all about making your server faster so that Google naturally prioritizes your site.

Core Web Vitals That Matter for Crawling

Google uses three main Core Web Vitals metrics to assess user experience, and guess what? Each one also plays a role in crawling indirectly.

Largest Contentful Paint (LCP)

LCP is all about when the main content of a page loads. Google aims for this to happen in under 2.5 seconds. 

Some common culprits for slow LCP include:

Hero images that aren't optimized
Slow server response times
Render-blocking CSS or JavaScript
Font loading delays

If your LCP is consistently over 4 seconds, Google may deprioritize those pages in its crawl queue. So, speeding this up can help Google find your content faster!

First Input Delay (FID) / Interaction to Next Paint (INP)

FID measures how responsive your site is, with Google targeting under 100ms. In 2026, INP will take over FID.

JavaScript-heavy sites can struggle here. If your site is loading a ton of scripts before it's interactive, users and Googlebot alike will have to wait. Googlebot doesn't click buttons, but heavy JavaScript can block it from seeing all your content.

Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)

CLS measures how stable your layout is. Google aims for under 0.1 here.

While shifting layouts don't directly impact crawling, they can indicate missing dimensions on images or ads, which might confuse Googlebot when rendering your page.

Practical Speed Upgrades That Actually Work

I've tried a lot of speed optimization tips, and honestly, some of them were just noise. Here are the strategies that really made a difference for me:

Enable Compression

If your server isn't using Brotli or Gzip compression, your HTML, CSS, and JavaScript files might be way larger than they need to be 3-5 times larger, in fact! Enabling compression at the server level is crucial. If you're using a managed hosting service like Vercel, this is usually taken care of for you. If you're managing your own server, just double-check that compression is active.

You can test this using your browser's developer tools. Look for the response headers to see if you've got  (Brotli) or . If not, it's time to fix that before moving on!

Use Modern Image Formats

JPEG and PNG are a bit outdated. Formats like WebP and AVIF can provide the same quality at 25-30% smaller file sizes. I converted all the cover images on my site to WebP, and the average file size dropped from 120KB to 35KB without sacrificing quality.

For images in your content, always specify explicit width and height attributes. This helps prevent layout shifts and improves LCP.

Eliminate Render-Blocking Resources

You might have seen the "Eliminate render-blocking resources" warning in Google's PageSpeed Insights. These are CSS or JavaScript files that need to load before anything shows up for users.

To fix this, you can inline critical CSS, defer non-critical CSS, and use  or  attributes on JavaScript files. If you're using modern frameworks like Astro (which powers this site), it does a lot of this automatically for you.

Set Proper Cache Headers

Cache headers guide browsers and CDNs on how long to keep copies of your files. For example, if your CSS file takes 200ms to download, you don't want the browser to download it again on the next page load.

You can set  for static assets like CSS, JavaScript, and images. For HTML pages that change more frequently, use . By fixing my cache headers, I managed to reduce repeat-visit load times by 60%.

Optimize Server Response Time

TTFB is the time from when a browser requests a page to when it receives the first byte of the response. For dynamic sites, this includes database queries, template rendering, and framework boot time.

If your site uses a database, it's worth checking query performance. A slow query can add a hefty delay to your load times. Look int

---

## GA4 Events: Automatic, Recommended, and Custom

**Pillar:** Website Setup & SEO
**URL:** https://www.praveentechworld.com/blog/ga4-events-automatic-recommended-custom-tracking-guide
**Published:** 2026-06-12

> Automatic, recommended, or custom GA4 events - learn the difference and how to track button clicks, form submissions, file downloads, and scroll depth.

Have you ever felt a bit lost when diving into GA4' I know I did! During my first month exploring Google Analytics 4, I was completely overwhelmed by the variety of events available. Some popped up automatically, while others needed a bit of manual setup, and there were also those that Google recommended but didn't enforce. Figuring out which ones to use and when was quite a puzzle.

But don't worry! After some digging, I realized it's not as complicated as it first seems. GA4 categorizes events into three main types: automatic, recommended, and custom. Each type has its own unique purpose.

Automatic Events: The Freebies

Let's kick things off with automatic events. These are the events that GA4 tracks without you needing to lift a finger. They start recording as soon as you install the GA4 tag. Here's what you can expect them to capture:

: every time someone loads a page
: each new session that begins
: the very first time a user visits your site
: when a page is in focus for over 10 seconds

These events take care of the essentials for you. You'll get page view counts and session information without having to mess with any code. Plus, if you've enabled enhanced measurement (which I highly recommend), you can also track events like , , , , and .

One thing to keep in mind: you can't modify automatic events. You won't be able to add custom parameters or change their firing behavior. They form the backbone of your GA4 data, and everything else builds on top of them.

If you're not seeing these events, it might be worth checking your enhanced measurement settings. You can find that under Admin Data Streams > your web stream. For more details on nailing the basics, check out the GA4 page tracking guide.

Recommended Events: The Template

Now, let's talk about recommended events. These are predefined events that Google suggests based on different industries and use cases. They come with standardized names and parameters that help unlock built-in reports.

If you're running an ecommerce site, for instance, Google recommends events like:
: when someone views a product
: when a product is added to a cart
: when a transaction is completed
: when a product is taken out of the cart

If your site focuses on content or lead generation, you might want to consider:
: when someone submits a form
: when someone creates an account
: when content is shared via social media
